misconceptions: A student might believe that 11 is just 'one and one' meaning two ones, not understanding that it is one ten and one one.

core_concept: 2D and 3D Shapes: Names, Attributes, and Composing Simple Shapes
facts: A square has 4 sides that are all the same length and 4 corners, not like a triangle which has 3 sides.
A cube has 6 flat faces, while a sphere has 0 flat faces because its surface is completely round.
misconceptions: A shape that is tilted or turned is a different shape—learners think a rotated square is a diamond, not a square, because they focus on orientation rather than sides and corners.

core_concept: Putting Together and Taking Away
facts: Putting two groups together always gives a larger total than either group alone; for example, 2 blocks and 3 blocks put together make 5 blocks, which is more than 2 and more than 3.
Taking away objects from a group gives a smaller total; if you have 5 grapes and eat 2, only 3 grapes are left, which is less than 5.
The action of putting together to find a total is called adding, and the action of taking away to find what is left is called subtracting; these words name what happens to the group.
misconceptions: When two groups are put together, some children believe the total is just the number of objects in the larger group, ignoring the smaller group entirely; for example, with 3 blocks and 2 blocks, they say the total is 3, not 5.

core_concept: Same and Different: Sorting and Classifying
facts: Two objects are the same color when they both have that color, like a red apple and a red ball — they belong in a group of red things.
Sorting means putting objects into groups by noticing one shared attribute, such as putting all square blocks together and all round blocks together.
An object can be different from another object in size even if they are the same shape; a big circle and a small circle are different in size but same in shape.
misconceptions: A learner might think that objects can only be considered 'same' if they match in every possible way (color, shape, and size), not understanding that 'same' can refer to just one shared attribute like color.

core_concept: Simple Bar Graphs and Organizing Data
facts: In a bar graph, the tallest bar always shows the group with the most items, and the shortest bar shows the group with the fewest.
When data is organized into a bar graph, you can find which group has more or fewer by looking at how high each bar is, without counting each item again.
To make a bar graph, first count how many items are in each group, then draw a bar that is that many units tall to record the count.
misconceptions: A child might think that a bar with a brighter color or a larger picture inside has more items, even if it is shorter than another bar; but the height of the bar alone tells which group has more.

core_concept: Money: Coins and Bills
facts: A penny is worth 1 cent and a quarter is worth 25 cents; because 25 is more than 1, a quarter has more value than a penny.
A dollar bill is paper money, not a coin; it has more value than a single quarter because you can exchange one dollar for four quarters, but one quarter cannot buy as much as a dollar.
Value tells how much something is worth; a coin with a larger number (like 25 for a quarter) has more value than a coin with a smaller number (like 1 for a penny).
You need 100 pennies to equal the value of one dollar bill, but one penny alone is not worth a dollar; this shows the dollar bill has more value than a single penny.
misconceptions: A penny is worth more than a dollar bill because the penny is made of metal and feels heavier, so it must be more valuable.
A quarter is worth the same as a penny because both are coins and you can use them the same way to buy things.

core_concept: Measurement and Comparison: Longer, Taller, Heavier
facts: A longer object stretches farther from one end to the other than a shorter object, so a pencil is longer than a crayon if it reaches past the crayon's tip when lined up.
A taller object reaches higher from the ground than a shorter one, so a giraffe is taller than a dog because the giraffe's head is higher up.
A heavier object feels harder to lift than a lighter one, so a full backpack is heavier than an empty backpack because it has more things inside.
A full cup has liquid up to the top, while an empty cup has no liquid inside, so a cup with water to the rim is full and a cup with no water is empty.
misconceptions: A child believes a taller container always holds more liquid than a shorter one, even when the shorter container is wider and actually holds more.
A child thinks a big object is always heavier than a small object, so they say a large empty box is heavier than a small rock, even though the rock is heavier to lift.

core_concept: Patterns: Recognizing, Continuing, and Creating
facts: In an AB pattern, the two items alternate; after red comes blue, then red again, so the next after blue must be red because the pattern repeats every two items.
To continue a pattern, you look at the last two items; if you see red then blue, the next is red because the rule is 'alternate' and the sequence starts over.
A pattern uses a rule that repeats; for example, the rule 'circle then square' creates circle-square-circle-square, and you can create the same pattern with different objects like colors or sounds.
misconceptions: A student might think a pattern like 'red, blue' continues by repeating only the first color (red, blue, red, red, red...) because they ignore the alternation and just repeat the first element they see.

core_concept: Counting to Ten and Ordinal Position
facts: The numeral '5' is the written symbol for the spoken number word 'five', and it always represents that many objects when counting a set.
When objects are placed in a line, the first object is the one at the very beginning, and the last object is the one at the very end, no matter how many objects are in between.
A bar graph shows data by using bars of different lengths; a bar that reaches the number 6 on the side means six objects were counted for that category.
The ordinal word 'first' means the object in position 1, not the object with the most or least of something.
misconceptions: A child counts the same object twice when counting a set of six objects because they do not track which objects have already been counted.
A child says the last object counted is 'first' because it is the one they touched last, confusing the order of touching with the ordinal position.

core_concept: Small Quantities: One to Four, More and Less
facts: When counting a group of up to four objects, the last number you say tells how many objects are in the group; for example, if you count 'one, two, three' and stop, there are three objects.
To compare two groups, you can match each object in one group to exactly one object in the other group; if every object has a partner and none are left over, the groups have the same number.
A group of four objects has more objects than a group of two objects because four comes after two when counting, and you can see that two is less than four.
misconceptions: A child may believe that a group of objects spread out over a larger area has more objects than a group of the same number of objects clustered together, because the spread-out group looks bigger.

core_concept: Basic Shapes: Circle, Square, Triangle, Rectangle
facts: A circle has a curved outline with no corners, unlike a square which has four straight sides and four corners.
A triangle has exactly three straight sides and three corners; a rectangle has four straight sides and four corners.
A square is a special kind of rectangle because it has four equal sides and four corners, but not all rectangles are squares.
A rectangle has two pairs of same-length straight sides; the shorter pair and the longer pair are different lengths unless it is a square.
misconceptions: A triangle and a rectangle both have four corners — learners may think all shapes with straight sides have the same number of corners.
Any shape with a curved outline is called a circle — learners may mistake an oval or a rounded blob for a circle because it is not pointy.

core_concept: Sun, Moon, and Stars: Predictable Patterns
facts: The Sun rises in the east and sets in the west because Earth rotates from west to east, making the Sun appear to move across the sky.
The Moon changes shape in a repeating pattern called phases, from new moon to full moon and back, over about 29 days.
Day and night happen because Earth rotates, so the side facing the Sun has day while the side away from the Sun has night.
Stars are visible at night but not during the day because the Sun's bright light in the sky hides them from our view.
misconceptions: Students think the Sun moves across the sky because it is traveling around Earth, not because Earth is rotating.
Students believe the Moon's shape changes because clouds cover parts of it, not because of the changing angle of sunlight hitting the Moon.

core_concept: Human Body Systems: Skeletal, Muscular, Respiratory, Circulatory, Nervous
facts: Bones are hard and give your body shape; without bones you would be floppy like a jellyfish.
Muscles pull on bones to make your arms and legs move; when you bend your arm, a muscle on the front gets shorter.
Your heart is a pump that pushes blood through your body; you can feel it beat faster when you run.
Your lungs take in air when you breathe; they are like balloons that fill up and then let air out.
Your brain is the boss that sends messages through nerves to tell your muscles what to do.
misconceptions: Muscles push bones to make them move, like pushing a toy car.
The heart is shaped like a valentine and its job is to make you feel love.

core_concept: Exploring Light and Sound
facts: A shadow forms when an object blocks light because light travels in straight lines and cannot bend around the object.
Sound is created by vibrations; for example, a drum makes a sound because the drumhead vibrates, and if you touch it to stop the vibration, the sound stops.
Light can be reflected off shiny surfaces like mirrors, which is why you can see your reflection, but rough dark surfaces absorb light and appear dark.
Sound travels through solids, liquids, and gases; you can hear a sound through a closed door because the vibrations travel through the door material.
misconceptions: Students believe that shadows are made of dark material or that a shadow is a separate object that follows them, rather than understanding that a shadow is just the absence of light where an object blocks it.
Students think sound can only travel through air and not through solid objects like a table or wall, so they are surprised when they hear a tap through a table by putting their ear to it.

core_concept: Simple Machines
facts: A lever uses a long stick that rocks on a point called the fulcrum; pushing down on one end lifts the other end, helping you move a heavy object with less effort.
A wheel and axle work together like on a bicycle; turning the big wheel makes the small axle turn, which helps move you forward.
A ramp is a slanted surface that lets you roll a heavy object up instead of lifting it straight up; the longer the ramp, the less force you need to push.
A pulley is a wheel with a rope around it; pulling down on the rope lifts the load up, so you can use your weight to help lift.
misconceptions: A longer ramp makes it harder to push an object up because you have to push a longer distance; in fact, a longer ramp reduces the force needed to lift the object.
A pulley makes the load lighter because you pull down instead of up; actually, a fixed pulley only changes the direction of your pull, not the amount of force needed.

core_concept: Plant and Animal Survival: Structure, Function, Growth
facts: A bird uses its beak to get food, so the beak is a structure that helps the bird survive.
A plant's stem holds it up and carries water to the leaves, so the stem has the function of support and transport.
A baby dog looks like its parents because offspring resemble their parents, not other kinds of animals.
A turtle pulls into its shell when it feels danger, showing that a stimulus causes a response to help it survive.
misconceptions: Students think all baby animals look exactly like their parents from birth, but some, like caterpillars, change form as they develop.
Students believe a plant's only job is to grow, not that its parts have specific functions like taking in water or making food.

core_concept: Human Body: Cells, Tissues, Digestion, Excretion
facts: The stomach is an organ made of several types of tissues, and each tissue is a group of similar cells that work together to digest food.
The small intestine absorbs nutrients from digested food into the blood because its inner surface has tiny finger-like bumps that capture the nutrients.
The kidneys are part of the excretory system; they filter waste from the blood to make urine, which leaves the body through the bladder and urethra.
Cells are the smallest living parts of your body; for example, your skin is made of skin cells, and these cells form a tissue that protects your body.
misconceptions: Students think that the stomach is the only organ that digests food, and that the small intestine just moves food along without doing anything important.
Students think that cells are only found in plants or in pictures, not in their own muscles, skin, and bones.

core_concept: Electricity and Magnetism
facts: A circuit needs a complete loop from one end of the battery, through wires and a device (like a light bulb), back to the other end of the battery, or else electricity cannot flow.
Magnets have two poles, north and south; opposite poles attract each other, but like poles (north-north or south-south) push each other away.
Electricity can be dangerous; you should never put metal objects or fingers into electrical outlets because the electricity can travel through your body and hurt you.
A switch is a part of a circuit that can open or close the path; when the switch is open, the circuit is broken and the device stops working.
misconceptions: Students think that a light bulb will light if only one wire connects it to a battery, because they believe electricity just flows out of the battery into the bulb, but a complete circuit is needed for electricity to flow.
Students believe that all magnets attract each other, so putting two magnets with the same poles together will make them stick, but like poles actually repel and push apart.

core_concept: Organisms and Their Habitats: Plants, Animals, Ecosystems
facts: A habitat is the place where an organism lives and gets everything it needs, like food, water, and shelter; a desert is a habitat for a cactus because the cactus can store water.
Plants and animals in an ecosystem depend on each other; for example, bees pollinate flowers to get nectar, and the flowers need bees to make seeds.
A food chain shows who eats whom; a rabbit that eats grass is prey for a fox, and the fox is the predator that eats the rabbit.
Different habitats have different plants and animals because each organism is suited to its habitat; a fish has gills to breathe in water, not on land.
Diversity means many different kinds of living things; a rainforest has high diversity with many species, while a desert has lower diversity with fewer species.
misconceptions: Students think a habitat is just a place where an animal lives, not realizing it must also provide everything the organism needs to survive, like food and water.
Students believe that if one animal eats another, the eaten animal is always bad or weak, rather than understanding that being prey is a natural role in a food chain.

core_concept: Exploring Land and Water: Landforms, Earth's Water, Erosion
facts: Most of Earth's surface is covered by saltwater oceans, not freshwater lakes or rivers.
Wind and water slowly wear away rock and soil in a process called erosion; for example, a river can carve a deep canyon over thousands of years.
Mountains are tall landforms with steep sides, while valleys are low areas between mountains or hills.
A delta forms where a river deposits soil and sand as it enters a lake or ocean, creating a fan-shaped landform.
misconceptions: Students think that most of Earth's water is freshwater that people can drink, not realizing that over 97% of Earth's water is salty ocean water.
Students think that erosion happens very quickly, like a landslide or a wave washing away sand in minutes, rather than slowly over many years.

core_concept: Properties of Matter: States, Heating, Cooling, Building
facts: Water can exist as a solid (ice), a liquid (water), or a gas (water vapor); when ice melts or water freezes, it is still the same substance — water — just in a different state.
When liquid water evaporates, it becomes water vapor, an invisible gas that mixes with the air; the water does not disappear, it changes state.
Solids like a wooden block keep their shape because their particles are packed tightly; liquids like water take the shape of their container because their particles can slide past each other.
You can describe a rock by its properties: it is hard, rough, and grey; different materials have different properties that help decide what to build with them.
misconceptions: When water evaporates, it turns into nothing and disappears forever.
Ice and water are different substances because ice is cold and solid, while water is liquid.

core_concept: Human Body: Senses and Movement
facts: The muscular system and skeletal system work together to move the body because muscles pull on bones, not because they push.
The eye is a complex sensory organ that detects light, not sound, and sends signals to the nervous system for vision.
The ear is a complex sensory organ that detects vibrations in the air, not light, and sends signals to the nervous system for hearing.
The nervous system coordinates muscles and senses by sending signals from the brain to the body, not by muscles acting on their own.
Glasses correct eyesight by bending light before it enters the eye, not by changing the shape of the eye itself.
misconceptions: Students think muscles can push bones to move them, but muscles only pull on bones to create movement.
Students believe the ear hears by detecting light or that the eye sees by detecting sound, when each organ is specialized for a different type of signal.

core_concept: Investigating Forces: Motion, Friction, and Magnetism
facts: A force is a push or a pull that can start an object moving, stop it, or change its direction; without a force, a stationary object stays still.
Friction is a force that slows moving objects because their surfaces rub together; a ball rolls farther on a smooth floor than on rough carpet because the carpet has more friction.
Magnetism is a force that attracts only certain metals like iron and steel—not all metals; a magnet can pick up a paperclip but not an aluminum can because aluminum is not magnetic.
When two objects collide, the force from the collision can change their motion; a moving toy car hitting a stationary one makes the stationary car move forward while the moving car slows down.
You can predict motion by comparing forces: a harder push makes a toy car move faster and farther than a gentle push because stronger forces cause greater changes in motion.
misconceptions: Many students believe that a moving object must have a continuous force pushing it in the direction of motion, when in reality once it is moving it can keep going until friction or another force slows it down.
Students often think that magnets attract all metals, so they expect a magnet to stick to a copper penny or an aluminum foil, but only metals like iron and steel are attracted by magnets.

core_concept: Habitats and Change: Ecosystems Over Time
facts: A fossil is evidence of an organism that lived long ago, not a rock that was always there, because it formed when the organism's remains were buried and replaced by minerals over millions of years.
An ecosystem can change when humans build roads or cut down trees, even though the land looks different afterward, because living things must adapt or move to survive the new conditions.
Some animals that lived long ago, like dinosaurs, are extinct because their habitats changed too quickly for them to adapt, not because they were all killed by a single event.

Rock layers can show how an environment changed over time because different layers contain different fossils; for example, a layer with sea shell fossils below a layer with land plant fossils shows that the area was once under water and later became land.
When a volcano erupts or a fire burns a forest, the ecosystem changes naturally, but plants and animals that survive can slowly return and rebuild the ecosystem over many years.
misconceptions: Students think that fossils are the actual remains of animals, not rock copies that formed over millions of years.
Students believe that habitats only change when people do something, not that natural events like floods or volcanic eruptions can also change them.

core_concept: Life Cycles, Traits, and Variations
facts: Life cycles differ among organisms: a butterfly goes through egg, larva, pupa, and adult (metamorphosis), while a dog is born as a puppy and grows directly into an adult without a pupa stage.
Traits like fur color or leaf shape are inherited from parents through reproduction, meaning they are passed down from one generation to the next, not caused by the environment.
Variation occurs because offspring receive a combination of traits from both parents; for example, a puppy may have its mother's ear shape but its father's tail length.
A trait that helps an organism survive in its environment is called a survival advantage; for instance, a cactus has thick stems to store water in a dry desert, which helps it live longer.

The environment can influence how a trait is expressed without changing the inherited traits; for example, two identical sunflower seeds may grow to different heights if one gets more sunlight, but both still have the same inherited ability to grow.
misconceptions: Students often think that all animal life cycles include a metamorphosis stage like butterflies, not realizing that many animals (e.g., dogs, birds) grow directly from baby to adult without changing form.

Students may believe that if a parent loses a body part (like a tail) during its life, its offspring will be born without that part, instead of understanding that such acquired changes are not inherited.

core_concept: Weather and Climate
facts: The atmosphere is a layer of gases around Earth that holds the air we breathe and moves to create wind, which carries heat and moisture from one place to another.
Climate is the average weather pattern of a region over 30 years or more, not the daily temperature or rain you see in a forecast.
The water cycle moves water from the ground to the sky and back again: when the sun heats water, it evaporates into the atmosphere, then cools and falls as precipitation like rain or snow.
A weather forecast uses observations of temperature, wind, and clouds to predict what the atmosphere will do in the next few hours or days, not what the climate will be like in 10 years.
Hazardous weather such as thunderstorms, tornadoes, or blizzards can be predicted by forecasters, and people can prepare by staying indoors, having supplies ready, or following safety warnings.
misconceptions: Students think climate and weather are the same thing, so they say 'the climate is rainy today' instead of understanding that climate describes long-term patterns, not a single day's conditions.
Students believe precipitation only means rain, ignoring that snow, sleet, and hail are also forms of precipitation that fall from the atmosphere during the water cycle.

core_concept: Human Body: Respiration and Circulation
facts: The heart pumps blood through blood vessels to deliver oxygen to all cells, not just to the lungs.
When you breathe faster during exercise, your lungs take in more oxygen so your blood can carry more oxygen to your working muscles.
Carbon dioxide is a waste gas that your cells produce; the blood carries it to the lungs, where you breathe it out, not back into the body.
The lungs and heart work together: the lungs add oxygen to the blood, and then the heart pumps that oxygen-rich blood to the rest of the body.
misconceptions: Students believe that the heart pumps air around the body because they feel it beating in their chest and associate it with breathing.
Students think that oxygen travels directly from the lungs to the cells without any help from the heart or blood vessels.

core_concept: Energy Transfer and Transformation
facts: Kinetic energy is the energy of motion; a rolling ball has kinetic energy because it is moving, while a stationary ball has none.
Potential energy is stored energy; a ball held above the ground has gravitational potential energy because of its height, and it transforms into kinetic energy when dropped.
Thermal energy transfers from a warmer object to a cooler object; a metal spoon in hot soup gets warm because thermal energy moves from the soup to the spoon.
In a collision, energy is transferred between objects; when a moving toy car hits a stationary one, the stationary car starts moving because it gained kinetic energy from the collision.
Engineers use energy transformation to solve problems; a solar-powered calculator transforms light energy into electrical energy to work without batteries.
misconceptions: Students think energy is a substance that can be used up and disappear, rather than understanding that energy transfers or transforms but is not lost.
Students believe that only moving objects have energy and that a stationary object has no energy at all, ignoring stored potential energy.

core_concept: Processes That Shape Earth
facts: Earth's three main layers are the crust (the thin outer layer we live on), the mantle (a thick layer of hot, slowly flowing solid rock), and the core (the very hot center made of metal); the crust is the thinnest layer, like the skin of an apple.

Volcanic eruptions happen when magma from the mantle rises through cracks in the crust; the lava that cools on the surface adds new rock and slowly builds up mountains over time, showing how volcanism shapes the land.

Earthquakes occur when blocks of crust suddenly slip past each other, releasing energy that shakes the ground; they can cause buildings to collapse and affect people living nearby, making them a geologic event that impacts communities.

Erosion is the wearing away of Earth's surface by water, wind, or ice; over millions of years, it can carve deep canyons and wear down mountains into gentle hills, so the same process that happens slowly can create dramatic landscapes.

Over billions of years, geological processes like uplift (raising land) and erosion (wearing it down) have continuously reshaped Earth's surface, meaning the mountains we see today are not the same ones that existed long ago.
misconceptions: Students believe the mantle is entirely liquid because volcanoes erupt molten rock, not realizing the mantle is mostly solid rock that flows very slowly over long timescales.
Students think erosion happens quickly, like a single flood carving a canyon, when in fact most erosion takes millions or billions of years to significantly alter the landscape.

core_concept: Natural Resources for Energy
facts: Sunlight and wind are renewable energy resources because they are naturally replenished every day, unlike coal and oil which take millions of years to form.
Fossil fuels such as coal, oil, and natural gas are nonrenewable because they were formed from ancient organisms over millions of years and are used up much faster than they can be replaced.
Burning fossil fuels releases pollution like carbon dioxide and soot, which can harm health and the environment, whereas solar and wind energy produce no pollution during use.
Solar panels capture sunlight and convert it into electricity, and wind turbines use wind to spin a generator; both provide energy without burning any fuel.
Using renewable resources for energy is sustainable because we can keep using them without running out, while nonrenewable resources will eventually be exhausted.
misconceptions: Students may think that renewable resources like solar and wind energy can run out if used too much, confusing 'renewable' with 'unlimited' or believing that using energy depletes the source itself.
Students may believe that because fossil fuels come from nature, burning them is harmless, ignoring the pollution released during combustion.

core_concept: Structures and Functions of Living Things
facts: The eye's lens focuses light onto the back of the eye, which is why we can see sharp images; if the lens is misshapen, images become blurry.
The ear's eardrum vibrates when sound waves hit it, and these vibrations are passed to tiny bones inside the ear, allowing us to hear.
A stimulus like a loud noise triggers a response like covering your ears because the ear sends a signal through a pathway of nerves to the brain, which then tells muscles to act.
The eye and ear are sensory organs with specialized structures—such as the lens and eardrum—that each perform a specific function to detect light or sound, not just general sensing.

An organism's response to a stimulus is not possible without a pathway; for example, when you touch a hot surface, your hand pulls away because nerves carry the signal from skin to muscles via the spinal cord.
misconceptions: Students believe that the eye sees objects directly without any lens focusing, thinking the image is already clear as soon as they open their eyes.
Students think that hearing is just sound waves traveling into the ear and instantly being understood by the brain, missing the role of the eardrum and tiny bones in vibrating and transmitting sound.

core_concept: Investigating Waves: Sound, Light, and Information
facts: A wave transfers energy from one place to another without moving matter; for example, dropping a stone in water creates ripples that spread outward, but the water does not travel all the way to the edge.
Sound waves are created when an object vibrates, like a drumhead or guitar string, and those vibrations travel through air or other materials to reach your ear.
Light waves can travel through empty space, unlike sound waves, which need a medium such as air, water, or solid materials to travel through.

The amplitude of a wave is the distance from the center to the top of the wave; for sound waves, a larger amplitude means a louder sound, and for light waves, a larger amplitude means a brighter light.
People use waves to send information: radio stations send music using electromagnetic waves, and telephones convert your voice into electrical waves that travel long distances.
misconceptions: Students often think that when a stone is dropped into water, the water itself moves outward from the stone to the edge of the pond, carrying the wave.
Many students believe that sound can travel through empty space because they see astronauts talking to each other in space movies.

core_concept: Astronomy: Space Systems
facts: Earth's orbit around the Sun takes one year, and it is the tilt of Earth's axis, not the distance from the Sun, that causes the seasons because different hemispheres receive more direct sunlight at different times.
The Moon orbits Earth about once every 29.5 days, which is why its appearance changes from new to full and back over the course of a month.
Gravity is the force that pulls objects toward each other; it keeps planets in orbit around the Sun and moons around planets, and without it objects would fly off in straight lines.
Stars are giant balls of hot gas that produce their own light; the Sun is a medium-sized star, and other stars look small only because they are much farther away from Earth.
A galaxy is a huge collection of stars, gas, and dust held together by gravity; our solar system is just one tiny part of the Milky Way galaxy, which contains billions of stars.

Earth's axis is an imaginary line through the North and South Poles that is tilted at about 23.5 degrees, and this tilt causes the Sun's path across the sky to change height and length throughout the year, creating seasons.
misconceptions: Students believe Earth's seasons happen because Earth is closer to the Sun in summer and farther in winter, not because of the tilt of its axis.
Students think that gravity is a force that only exists on Earth and not in space, so they assume planets and stars float without any force holding them in orbit.
Students often confuse a galaxy and a solar system, thinking that our solar system is the entire galaxy or that galaxies are just groups of planets orbiting a single star.

core_concept: Human Body: Hormones and Reproduction
facts: Hormones are chemical messengers released by endocrine glands; they travel through the bloodstream to tell specific organs when to grow or change, such as a growth hormone telling bones to lengthen during childhood.

Puberty begins when the brain's hypothalamus signals the pituitary gland to release hormones that cause physical changes; for example, testosterone in boys causes voice deepening and facial hair, while estrogen in girls triggers breast development and menstruation.

The pituitary gland is often called the 'master gland' because it produces hormones that control other glands, like the thyroid or adrenal glands, which in turn regulate metabolism and stress responses.

Reproduction requires the production of egg and sperm cells, which are controlled by hormones; in females, the hormone estrogen helps mature an egg each month, and in males, testosterone stimulates sperm production.

Hormones differ from nerves in that hormones are chemical and travel slowly through blood, while nerves use electrical signals and travel quickly; for example, a hormone like adrenaline takes seconds to start working, whereas a nerve reflex happens instantly.
misconceptions: Many students believe that puberty begins at the same age for everyone and that it is caused by external factors like diet or exercise, rather than by internal hormonal signals from the endocrine system.
Students often think that hormones only affect emotions and mood, and do not realize that hormones also control physical growth, metabolism, and reproduction.

core_concept: Energy and Matter in Ecosystems
facts: Plants are called producers because they make their own food through photosynthesis, using sunlight, water, and carbon dioxide, unlike animals that must eat other organisms to get energy.
In a food chain, arrows show the direction of energy flow, not just 'who eats whom'; for example, an arrow from grass to rabbit means energy from the grass moves to the rabbit when it eats the grass.

Carbon cycles through ecosystems: plants take carbon dioxide from the air during photosynthesis, animals get carbon by eating plants, and both release carbon dioxide back into the air through respiration.
Nitrogen in the air cannot be used directly by plants; bacteria in the soil convert nitrogen gas into nitrates, which plants absorb and use to make proteins.
Decomposers like fungi and bacteria break down dead organisms and waste, returning nutrients like carbon and nitrogen to the soil and air, so they can be used again by plants.
Only about 10% of the energy stored in one level of a food chain is passed to the next level; the rest is used for life processes like growth, movement, and reproduction, or lost as heat.
misconceptions: Students think that plants get their food from the soil, not from sunlight and air, because they see plants growing in soil.
Students believe that energy, like matter, is recycled in an ecosystem, so the same energy is used over and over.
Students think that the arrow in a food chain simply means 'eats', so they draw arrows from predator to prey, opposite the correct direction of energy flow.

core_concept: Protecting Earth's Resources
facts: Conservation means using fewer resources so that they last longer; for example, turning off the tap while brushing teeth saves water for other uses.
Pollution from human activities, like dumping chemicals into rivers, can harm ecosystems by making the water unsafe for fish and plants.
Biodiversity is the variety of living things in an ecosystem; a forest with many different animals and plants is more resilient to damage than one with only a few species.
Sustainable practices, such as planting new trees after cutting old ones, help protect land resources by ensuring forests can regrow for future use.
Protecting Earth's resources like clean air and water is important because all living things, including humans, depend on healthy ecosystems to survive.
Recycling reduces the need to take new resources from the land; making a can from recycled aluminum uses less energy than making one from raw materials.
misconceptions: Students think that throwing trash in a bin means it disappears forever, not realizing that most trash goes to landfills where it can pollute land and water.
Students believe that cutting down trees is always bad for the environment, without understanding that sustainable logging with replanting can protect forest resources.
Students think that pollution only affects the place where it happens, not understanding that pollution can travel through air or water to harm distant ecosystems.

core_concept: Modeling Earth's Systems: Interacting Spheres
facts: The atmosphere is the layer of gases around Earth; it provides oxygen for living things and protects the planet from harmful solar radiation.
The hydrosphere includes all water on Earth—oceans, rivers, lakes, and groundwater—and it covers about 71% of the planet's surface, not just the water you can see.
The geosphere is made up of Earth's solid rock, soil, and landforms; when a volcano erupts, it releases lava and ash from the geosphere into the atmosphere and hydrosphere.
The biosphere is the part of Earth where living things exist; it depends on the atmosphere for air, the hydrosphere for water, and the geosphere for soil to grow plants.

A change in one sphere can cause a change in another sphere, not just stay in that sphere alone; for example, when rain (hydrosphere) falls on a mountain, it erodes rock (geosphere) and carries soil into rivers.
A model of Earth's systems shows how the four spheres interact; scientists use models to predict what happens when, say, more carbon dioxide enters the atmosphere from the geosphere's burning fuels.
misconceptions: The hydrosphere is only the oceans and lakes, not including water vapor in the air or water frozen in glaciers.
The geosphere is just the ground you walk on, not including mountains, volcanoes, or the soil that plants grow in.
The biosphere is only the animals, not including plants, fungi, and bacteria that also live and interact with other spheres.

core_concept: Investigating Matter: Properties, Structure, and Chemical Change
facts: Dissolving sugar in water is a physical change because the sugar molecules remain unchanged; you can taste the sweetness because the sugar is still there, just spread between water molecules.
Burning paper is a chemical change because the paper's molecules react with oxygen to form new substances like carbon dioxide and ash, which are different from the original paper.

An element consists of only one kind of atom, while a compound contains two or more different kinds of atoms bonded together; for example, oxygen gas (O2) is an element, but water (H2O) is a compound because it has both hydrogen and oxygen atoms.
A chemical formula, such as H2O, tells exactly which atoms are in a molecule and how many of each; H2O means two hydrogen atoms and one oxygen atom bonded together.
In a physical change like melting ice, the molecules themselves do not change; the H2O molecules in ice are the same as in liquid water, just arranged differently.

During a chemical change, atoms rearrange to form new molecules but are not created or destroyed; when wood burns, the atoms from the wood combine with oxygen atoms from the air to make carbon dioxide and water vapor.
misconceptions: Students believe that dissolving sugar in water is a chemical change because the sugar 'disappears,' not realizing it is a physical change where the sugar molecules remain intact.
Students believe that burning paper is a physical change because the paper changes shape and color, but it is actually a chemical change because new substances form.
Students believe that a compound like water is a mixture of hydrogen and oxygen that can be separated by filtering or evaporating, not understanding that the atoms are chemically bonded.

core_concept: Cells and Systems
facts: Cells are the basic building blocks of all living things; a single-celled organism like an amoeba is a complete living thing made of just one cell, while a human is made of trillions of cells.
Mitosis is a type of cell division that produces two identical daughter cells, enabling an organism to grow and repair damaged tissue such as skin after a cut.
Tissues are groups of similar cells that work together to perform a specific function; for example, muscle tissue is made of muscle cells that contract to move parts of the body.
Organs are structures made of two or more different types of tissues working together; the stomach contains muscle tissue for churning food and epithelial tissue for lining its inner surface.

An organ system is a collection of organs that work together to carry out a major life process; the digestive system includes the stomach, intestines, and liver to break down food and absorb nutrients.
All new cells arise from existing cells through division; when a bone breaks, cells in the bone divide (mitosis) to repair the fracture.
misconceptions: Cells exist only in animals and humans; plants are not made of cells.
Growth occurs because individual cells swell up like balloons, not because cells divide to form more cells.
An organ like the heart is made entirely of one kind of tissue, such as only muscle tissue.

core_concept: Natural Hazards
facts: A hurricane is a type of hazardous weather that forms over warm ocean water, not over land, because it needs heat and moisture from the ocean to strengthen.
Earthquakes and volcanoes are natural hazards caused by movements of Earth's tectonic plates, not by weather conditions like wind or rain.
Landslides happen when soil or rock on a slope becomes unstable, often after heavy rain or an earthquake, not because the ground suddenly appears without warning.
Floods occur when water overflows onto normally dry land, and they can be caused by heavy rain, storm surges from hurricanes, or rapid snowmelt, not just by broken dams.
Human societies can prepare for natural hazards like tornadoes by building storm shelters and having warning systems, but they cannot prevent the tornado from forming.
misconceptions: Students think a hurricane is the same as a tornado because both are spinning wind storms, but hurricanes form over oceans and are much larger, while tornadoes form over land from thunderstorms.
Students believe earthquakes only happen in places with volcanoes, but earthquakes occur along fault lines anywhere tectonic plates meet, even where there are no volcanoes.

core_concept: Light and Matter: Electromagnetic Waves
facts: Light is an electromagnetic wave, not a mechanical wave like sound, so it can travel through the vacuum of space without needing a medium.

The electromagnetic spectrum includes radio, microwave, infrared, visible, ultraviolet, X-ray, and gamma waves, all differing in wavelength and frequency; visible light is only a narrow band between infrared and ultraviolet.
Infrared waves have longer wavelengths than visible light; they are emitted by warm objects and can be felt as heat, but they are not the same as the thermal motion of molecules.
Ultraviolet waves have shorter wavelengths than visible light and carry more energy, which is why they can damage skin cells and cause sunburn even on cloudy days.
Refraction occurs when light changes speed as it moves from one material to another, causing it to bend; for example, a straw in a glass of water appears bent because light slows down in water.
Reflection happens when light bounces off a surface; a mirror reflects most visible light because its smooth surface causes regular reflection, while rough surfaces scatter light.
misconceptions: Students believe that radio waves, microwaves, and X-rays are completely different from 'light' and are not electromagnetic waves, when in fact they are all part of the same electromagnetic spectrum.
Students think that light travels faster in water than in air because water is denser, but light actually slows down in water, causing the bending we call refraction.

Students assume that ultraviolet light is just a type of heat or that it only causes damage when it is bright, not recognizing that UV carries higher energy than visible light and can harm skin and eyes even in indirect sunlight.

core_concept: Plate Tectonics and Rock Cycling
facts: When two tectonic plates collide, the crust crumples and pushes upward, forming mountain ranges because the plates are too thick to slide under each other easily.
Earthquakes occur when tectonic plates sliding past each other get stuck and then suddenly slip, releasing stored energy as shaking; they don't happen when plates move smoothly.

Volcanoes commonly form at plate boundaries where one plate dives beneath another (subduction), because the sinking plate melts and creates magma that rises; a volcano is not a random burst from a flat plate interior.
Igneous rock forms when magma or lava cools and solidifies, not from compressed sediment or from heat-pressure change in solid rock.
Metamorphic rock is created when heat and pressure alter the minerals of an existing rock without melting it; for example, shale (sedimentary) turns into slate (metamorphic).

The rock cycle shows that any rock type can become another over time: for instance, sedimentary rock can be buried and heated to become metamorphic, which can then melt into magma to form igneous rock.
misconceptions: Students believe that earthquakes happen randomly anywhere on Earth, not realizing that most occur along tectonic plate boundaries where plates slide or collide.
Students think that all rocks form from cooled magma (igneous), so they assume sedimentary and metamorphic rocks are also from volcanoes.
Students believe that mountains form when the ground is pushed up from below by magma, rather than when tectonic plates collide and crumple the crust.

core_concept: Thermal Energy
facts: Thermal energy is the total kinetic energy of all particles in an object; a larger object at the same temperature has more thermal energy because it contains more particles.
Temperature measures the average kinetic energy of particles, not the total; a small hot pan can have a higher temperature than a large warm pool but less thermal energy.
Heat is the transfer of thermal energy from a hotter object to a cooler object, not a substance that flows into or out of an object.
Conduction transfers thermal energy through direct contact between particles; metals conduct better than wood because their particles are more closely packed and free electrons help move energy.
Convection transfers thermal energy by the movement of fluids: warmer, less dense fluid rises while cooler, denser fluid sinks, creating a current that redistributes energy.
Radiation transfers thermal energy through electromagnetic waves without needing a medium; the Sun's energy reaches Earth through radiation, not by conduction or convection.
misconceptions: Students think heat and temperature are the same, so they believe a large bucket of warm water has the same amount of heat as a small cup of hot water if they feel equally warm.
Students believe cold is a substance that moves from an ice cube into their hand, making the hand cold, rather than understanding that heat transfers from the hand to the ice.
Students think that in convection, heat itself rises (e.g., 'heat rises from a radiator'), when actually the warmer fluid rises because it is less dense, carrying thermal energy with it.

core_concept: Weather, Climate, and Water Cycling
facts: Evaporation is the process where liquid water turns into water vapor due to heat from the sun; this water vapor rises and is the first step in the water cycle that eventually leads to clouds and precipitation.
Condensation occurs when water vapor in the air cools and changes back into liquid droplets; these droplets gather to form clouds, so without condensation, clouds would not exist.
Precipitation happens when cloud droplets become heavy enough to fall as rain, snow, sleet, or hail; the type of precipitation depends on the temperature inside the cloud and the air below it.

Climate is the long-term average of weather conditions (temperature, precipitation, etc.) over at least 30 years, not the weather on a single day; for example, a desert has a dry climate even if it rains occasionally.

Latitude and oceans both affect climate: regions near the equator are warmer because they receive more direct sunlight, and coastal areas have milder temperatures than inland areas at the same latitude because water heats and cools more slowly than land.

Altitude affects climate: higher elevations are cooler than lower elevations at the same latitude because the air is thinner and holds less heat; this is why mountain peaks are often snow-capped even in tropical regions.
misconceptions: Climate and weather are the same thing; a rainy day means the region has a rainy climate.
Clouds are made of water vapor, not liquid water droplets.
Higher elevations are warmer because they are closer to the sun.

core_concept: Chemical Reactions and Matter: Atoms, Bonds, Reactions
facts: An atom's chemical identity depends on the number of protons in its nucleus; for example, any atom with 6 protons is carbon, and any atom with 8 protons is oxygen.

In a covalent bond, two atoms share one or more pairs of electrons, so both atoms are attracted to the same electrons; in an ionic bond, one atom transfers electrons to another, creating oppositely charged ions that attract each other.
During a chemical reaction, atoms are rearranged into new molecules, but no atoms are created or destroyed; the total number of each type of atom remains the same before and after.
A molecule is a neutral group of two or more atoms held together by covalent bonds; for example, a water molecule (H2O) has two hydrogen atoms and one oxygen atom bonded covalently.
Protons carry a positive electric charge and electrons carry a negative charge; opposite charges attract, which is why electrons are held near the nucleus and why atoms can bond.
Chemical bonds are not solid connectors but electrical attractions; for example, a covalent bond exists because the nuclei of both atoms are attracted to the same shared electrons.
misconceptions: Students believe that atoms are solid, indivisible spheres with no internal structure, not realizing they contain protons and electrons.
Students believe that when a substance burns, the atoms are destroyed and new atoms appear; for example, they think the ash weighs less because some atoms vanished.
Students think that in every chemical bond, electrons are shared equally between atoms; they do not recognize that in ionic bonds electrons are transferred, not shared.

core_concept: Matter Cycling and Photosynthesis
facts: Photosynthesis uses carbon dioxide from the air and water from the soil to produce glucose, which stores energy, and releases oxygen as a byproduct.
The carbon cycle moves carbon atoms from the atmosphere into plants through photosynthesis and back into the atmosphere when organisms respire or decompose.
In the nitrogen cycle, most organisms cannot use nitrogen gas from the air directly; it must first be converted into compounds like ammonia or nitrates by bacteria.
Cellular respiration and photosynthesis are complementary processes because the products of one (oxygen and glucose) are the reactants of the other (carbon dioxide and water).
Water cycles through evaporation, condensation, and precipitation, but plants also return water vapor to the air through transpiration, linking the water cycle to living systems.
Glucose produced in photosynthesis is a sugar that stores chemical energy, not just a building material; plants use it for growth and energy, not only for structure.
misconceptions: Students think plants get most of their mass from the soil, not from carbon dioxide in the air, because they see roots in dirt and don't realize carbon atoms come from CO2.

Students believe photosynthesis is the opposite of cellular respiration only in the sense that one happens in plants and the other in animals, missing that both occur in plants and are chemically linked.
Students think water in the water cycle only moves through evaporation and rain, ignoring that plants actively release water vapor through transpiration, which contributes to cloud formation.

core_concept: Ecosystem Dynamics
facts: In a predator-prey cycle, an increase in the prey population causes the predator population to rise because predators have more food; however, the rising predator population then reduces the prey population, leading to a subsequent decline in predators—this creates a repeating cycle, not a simple one-way effect.

A natural disturbance such as a drought can reduce the population of plants in an ecosystem, which then causes a decline in herbivore populations that depend on those plants, and subsequently a decline in predator populations that eat the herbivores—showing how disturbances ripple through the community.

Human activities like introducing a non-native predator can cause a sharp decline in native prey populations because the native prey have not evolved defenses against the new predator, unlike natural predator-prey interactions that have developed over time.

An ecosystem is not a collection of independent species; it is a dynamic system where changes in one population affect others through interactions such as predation and competition, and also affect the non-living environment.

After a disturbance like a fire, the ecosystem undergoes succession: first, pioneer species like grasses colonize the area, then shrubs, then trees, gradually rebuilding the community and population sizes over years.

The size of a population in an ecosystem is limited by available resources such as food and water; if a prey population grows too large, it may exhaust its food supply, leading to a crash even without predators.
misconceptions: Students believe that when a predator population increases, the prey population will continue to decrease indefinitely until the prey goes extinct, not understanding that the predator population will then decline due to lack of food, leading to a cycle.
Students think that after a forest fire, the ecosystem is permanently destroyed and no new plants or animals will return, when in fact ecosystems can recover through succession over time.

Students think that removing a predator from an ecosystem will always allow the prey population to grow without limit and remain high, not realizing that the prey population will then be limited by food supply and may crash.

core_concept: Chemical Reactions and Energy
facts: In an exothermic reaction like burning wood, chemical energy stored in the fuel is released as heat and light, which is why a fire feels warm.
In an endothermic reaction like photosynthesis, plants absorb energy from sunlight to convert carbon dioxide and water into food, storing chemical energy.
Chemical energy is the energy stored in the bonds of substances like fuel and food; when a reaction breaks those bonds, energy can be released or absorbed.
Burning gasoline in a car engine is an exothermic reaction because it releases energy, while cooking an egg requires absorbing energy from the stove, making it endothermic.
Food contains chemical energy that your body releases through digestion; this released energy powers your muscles and keeps you warm.
A reaction that feels cold to the touch, like dissolving certain salts in water, is endothermic because it absorbs energy from its surroundings.
misconceptions: Students think all chemical reactions release energy because they associate reactions with burning or explosions, but some reactions absorb energy and make things colder.
Students believe that if a reaction feels hot, it must be endothermic because heat is entering the substance, but exothermic reactions release heat outward, making the surroundings warmer.
Students think food gives you energy directly without a chemical reaction, not realizing that digestion involves chemical reactions that release the stored chemical energy.

core_concept: Metabolic Reactions and Cellular Respiration
facts: During cellular respiration, glucose and oxygen react to produce ATP, water, and carbon dioxide; the carbon dioxide is then exhaled from the lungs.
The glucose used in cellular respiration comes from the digestion of carbohydrates; without digestion, your cells would not have glucose for energy.
Oxygen is required for cellular respiration; without it, cells cannot efficiently produce ATP from glucose, and much less energy is available.
ATP (adenosine triphosphate) is the main energy carrier in cells; when a cell needs energy, it splits off one phosphate group from ATP, releasing energy for tasks like muscle contraction.
Enzymes are proteins that speed up metabolic reactions without being used up; for example, the enzyme amylase in saliva begins breaking down starch into sugars during digestion.
Metabolism includes all chemical reactions happening in your body, both building up molecules (like making proteins) and breaking them down (like cellular respiration).
misconceptions: Students believe that cellular respiration happens in the lungs because we breathe in oxygen and out carbon dioxide, but it actually occurs in every cell.
Students think that the energy from food is used directly by cells, without understanding that it must first be converted into ATP.
Students think that enzymes are consumed or destroyed during metabolic reactions, so the body must constantly replace them, but enzymes are recycled and reusable.

core_concept: Earth's Resources and Human Impact
facts: Fossil fuels like coal and oil are nonrenewable natural resources because they form over millions of years, unlike solar energy which is continuously replenished.
Burning fossil fuels releases carbon dioxide and other pollutants that trap heat in the atmosphere, making climate change worse rather than just a local air quality issue.
Renewable resources like sunlight and wind are available continuously, but their supply is not always reliable because weather and time of day affect availability.
Pollution from human activities—such as plastic waste in oceans—harms ecosystems not only by physical ingestion but also by releasing toxic chemicals into the food chain.
Sustainable use of natural resources means harvesting at a rate that ecosystems can replenish; for example, cutting timber slower than regrowth, not simply reducing consumption.

While individual actions like recycling and using public transit help lower pollution, large-scale environmental protection often requires government policies and new technologies because individual choices alone are insufficient to address global problems.
misconceptions: Fossil fuels will never run out because new oil and coal are constantly being formed deep underground at a fast rate.
Pollution from burning fossil fuels only affects the local area where it is released, not the global climate.
If every person recycles, we won't need to worry about overusing natural resources because recycling completely replaces the need for new materials.

core_concept: Atomic Structure and the Periodic Table
facts: The atomic number of an element equals the number of protons in its nucleus, which uniquely identifies the element; for example, every carbon atom has exactly 6 protons, and any atom with 6 protons is carbon.
The nucleus contains nearly all of an atom's mass because protons and neutrons are about 1,800 times heavier than electrons, so electrons contribute almost nothing to the mass.

Isotopes of the same element have the same number of protons but different numbers of neutrons; for instance, carbon-12 has 6 neutrons while carbon-14 has 8 neutrons, yet both are carbon because they have 6 protons.

The periodic table arranges elements in order of increasing atomic number, not atomic mass; this explains why tellurium (atomic number 52) comes before iodine (53) even though tellurium has a higher average atomic mass.

Elements in the same group (vertical column) of the periodic table have the same number of electrons in their outermost energy level, which gives them similar chemical reactivity; for example, all alkali metals (Group 1) react vigorously with water.
An atom is electrically neutral because the number of protons in the nucleus equals the number of electrons surrounding it; a sodium atom has 11 protons and 11 electrons, so its net charge is zero.

The first energy level of an atom can hold up to 2 electrons, and the second energy level can hold up to 8 electrons; this pattern explains why helium (2 electrons) is stable and neon (10 electrons) has a full outer level.
misconceptions: Students often think that the atomic number is the total number of particles in the nucleus (protons plus neutrons), confusing it with the mass number.
Students believe that all atoms of a given element have exactly the same mass, not realizing that isotopes exist with different numbers of neutrons.

Students assume that elements in the same row (period) of the periodic table have similar chemical properties because they are arranged horizontally, but actually properties repeat vertically in groups.

core_concept: DNA, Genetics, and Heredity
facts: DNA is shaped like a twisted ladder called a double helix; the rungs are made of base pairs (A-T and G-C), and the sides are sugar-phosphate backbones.
A gene is a specific segment of DNA that contains the instructions for making one protein, not the entire DNA molecule.

In Mendelian heredity, a dominant allele masks the effect of a recessive allele when both are present; for example, brown eyes are dominant over blue, so a person with one brown and one blue allele has brown eyes. However, dominance does not determine which trait is more common in a population.
A mutation is a change in the DNA sequence; it can be neutral (no effect), beneficial (improves survival), or harmful (causes disease), depending on where it occurs.
Chromosomes are tightly coiled structures of DNA; humans have 23 pairs, and each chromosome carries many genes.
Heredity is the passing of traits from parents to offspring through genes, not through acquired characteristics during life (such as skills or injuries).
The base pairing rules (A with T, G with C) are complementary, meaning if one strand has a sequence ATCG, the opposite strand must have TAGC.
misconceptions: All mutations are harmful and cause diseases.
A dominant trait must be more common in a population than a recessive trait.
Recessive traits disappear after one generation because they are masked by dominant traits.

core_concept: Evolution and Natural Selection
facts: Natural selection acts on existing genetic variation within a population, not on traits an individual acquires during its lifetime; a giraffe's neck length varies because of random mutations, not because it stretches its neck.
Darwin observed that finches on different Galapagos islands had beak shapes suited to the available food; this variation arose by chance, not because the birds needed different beaks.
Fossils preserved in rock layers show that species living millions of years ago had different forms than modern species, providing evidence that species change over time through natural selection.
Adaptations are inherited traits that improve an organism's chance of survival and reproduction in its environment; they develop over many generations, not within a single organism's life.
Genetic variation in a population comes from random mutations and sexual reproduction; without variation, natural selection has no different traits to favor or disfavor.
Homologous structures, such as the forelimb bones of humans, cats, whales, and bats, share a similar pattern despite different functions, indicating descent from a common ancestor with modifications.

Whether a trait is advantageous depends on the environment; a thick fur coat helps arctic animals survive cold but would cause overheating in a desert, so natural selection favors different traits in different conditions.
misconceptions: Students believe that an animal can pass on traits it developed during its lifetime to its offspring — for example, that a weightlifter's children will be born with bigger muscles.
Students think that a species develops a new trait specifically because it needs that trait to survive, such as fish growing legs because they need to live on land.
Students believe that natural selection always produces perfectly adapted organisms that are ideally suited to their environment, with no disadvantages or trade-offs.

core_concept: Waves, Electromagnetic Spectrum, and Electricity
facts: Waves transfer energy from one place to another without transferring matter; for example, a wave on a rope moves energy along the rope but the rope itself only moves up and down.

The electromagnetic spectrum arranges waves by frequency and wavelength; radio waves have the lowest frequency and longest wavelength, while gamma rays have the highest frequency and shortest wavelength.
In an electric circuit, voltage is the energy per unit charge that pushes current through a conductor; a higher voltage across the same resistor results in a larger current.
Ohm's Law states that current (I) equals voltage (V) divided by resistance (R); if you double the voltage while keeping resistance constant, the current doubles.
Visible light is only a small part of the electromagnetic spectrum; it lies between infrared and ultraviolet, and different colors correspond to different frequencies within that range.
Magnetism and electricity are linked: a changing magnetic field can induce an electric current in a wire, and an electric current creates a magnetic field around the wire.

The speed of all electromagnetic waves in a vacuum is the same (about 300,000 km/s), regardless of their frequency or wavelength; this means that radio waves and X-rays travel at the same speed in empty space.
misconceptions: Students often think that waves carry matter from one place to another, believing that a water wave moves water particles across the ocean, when in fact water particles mostly move in circles and the wave energy travels.

Students may believe that higher frequency electromagnetic waves (like X-rays) travel faster than lower frequency ones (like radio waves), because they think higher energy means faster speed, but in vacuum all EM waves travel at the same speed.

Students often think that a higher voltage always means a higher current, ignoring that current also depends on resistance; if resistance increases, current may not increase even if voltage is higher.

core_concept: Changing Environments
facts: An environment changes when the season changes; in winter, some places get cold and snow covers the ground, so animals like birds migrate to warmer places.
Living things like plants and animals can adapt to changes in their habitat; for example, a tree loses its leaves in autumn to survive the cold winter.
Non-living things in an environment, like rocks and water, do not move or grow, but they can change when the weather changes, such as water turning to ice in winter.
misconceptions: A child may think that all animals stay in the same place all year long, not realizing that some animals migrate to find food or warmer weather when the environment changes.

core_concept: Pushes, Pulls, and Magnets
facts: A push moves an object away from you, while a pull moves an object toward you.
A magnet attracts (pulls) objects made of iron, like a nail, but does not attract plastic or paper.
Two magnets can push apart (repel) when their same ends are near each other, or pull together (attract) when opposite ends face each other.
misconceptions: All metal objects are attracted to magnets.

core_concept: Needs of Plants and Animals
facts: Plants need sunlight, water, and soil to grow, but animals need food, water, and shelter instead of sunlight and soil.
A plant that gets no water will wilt and stop growing, while an animal without food or shelter cannot survive.
Both plants and animals are living things because they grow and need water to stay alive.
misconceptions: Students think animals need sunlight and soil to survive just like plants do, because both are living things.

core_concept: Human Body and Five Senses
facts: We see with our eyes because they take in light, but we hear with our ears because they take in sound — each sense has its own organ.
Our skin lets us feel touch, so we can tell if something is rough or smooth, but only our tongue can taste food.
We smell with our nose; for example, we know a flower is sweet because our nose detects the smell, not our tongue.
misconceptions: Students believe that they taste food with their nose because they smell it while eating, but only the tongue can detect taste.

core_concept: Weather Patterns and Sunlight
facts: Sunny weather feels warm because sunlight heats the ground and air.
Rain and snow are both types of weather, but rain is liquid water and snow is frozen ice crystals.
A storm can be dangerous because it may have strong wind, heavy rain, or lightning.
misconceptions: Students think cloudy weather is always cold, but clouds can block sunlight and still feel warm.

core_concept: Animal Life Cycles: Birth, Growth, Adult
facts: A kitten is born as a tiny, blind, and deaf baby that cannot walk, but it grows into an adult cat that can see, hear, and hunt because the kitten goes through a growth stage called development.
A chick hatches from an egg as a yellow, fluffy baby that looks very different from its parents, who have feathers, because the chick must grow and change before becoming an adult.
A puppy grows into a dog; adult dogs can have puppies of their own, which is part of the life cycle where young become adults and then have offspring.
misconceptions: A baby animal looks exactly like its parent, just smaller.

core_concept: Animals: Living Things with Bodies, Needs, and Homes
facts: Animals are living things, so they need food and water to stay alive; a rock or toy is not living and does not need food.
A fish is an animal that lives in water habitats like lakes or oceans, while a bird is an animal that lives on land in forests or farms.
Every animal has a body and can move: a bird moves by flying, a snake moves by slithering, and a fish moves by swimming.
misconceptions: Some children think that all animals live on land and have legs, so they do not consider fish or snakes to be animals.

core_concept: Caring for Resources: Recycling and Conservation
facts: Recycling a plastic bottle turns it into something new, like a toy or another bottle, so it does not become trash.
Conserving water by turning off the tap while brushing your teeth means less water goes down the drain and is not wasted.
Using a reusable lunch bag many times instead of a new paper bag each day means we use fewer resources and make less waste.
misconceptions: Children may think that recycling means throwing items into a special bin where they disappear forever, not realizing they are turned into new products.

core_concept: The Human Body: Parts, Needs, and Growth
facts: The heart pumps blood to all parts of the body; you can feel your heart beating faster when you run because your muscles need more blood.
Your lungs take in air when you breathe; the air gives your body the oxygen it needs to stay alive.
Humans grow from babies to children to adults over many years; babies cannot walk or talk, but with food and water they grow bigger and learn new things.
Your tongue is a sense organ that helps you taste; your eyes see, ears hear, nose smells, and skin feels touch.
misconceptions: Some children think the heart is for feeling love or emotions, but the heart's real job is to pump blood through the body.
Some children think babies grow into adults very quickly, like in a few days, but growing from a baby to an adult takes many years of eating, drinking, and sleeping.

core_concept: Living and Non-Living Things
facts: A plant is a living thing because it grows and needs water, even though it does not move from place to place like an animal.
An animal is a living thing because it can move on its own and needs food and water, while a toy only moves when someone pushes it.
Living things respond to their surroundings — for example, a flower turns toward the sun, but a plastic flower never changes direction.
misconceptions: A plant is not alive because it does not move.

core_concept: Magnets: Attract and Repel
facts: A magnet attracts some metals like iron and steel, but it does not attract wood, plastic, or paper because those materials are not magnetic.
When the north pole of one magnet faces the north pole of another magnet, they push each other away — that pushing away is called repulsion.
A magnet can pull an iron paperclip toward it even without touching it, because the magnetic force works through the air.
misconceptions: Children often think a magnet sticks to any shiny metal, like aluminum foil or copper pennies, because all metals look similar.

core_concept: Water, Air, and Light
facts: Water is a liquid that flows and takes the shape of its container, but when it gets very cold it becomes a solid called ice.
Air is all around us; we cannot see it but we can feel it as wind when it moves.
Light from the sun allows us to see things; when something blocks light, it makes a shadow.
misconceptions: Air is not real because you can't see it.

core_concept: Plants: Parts and What They Need
facts: A plant's roots take up water from the soil and anchor the plant so it does not fall over; without roots, a plant cannot stand up or drink.
A leaf collects sunlight to help the plant grow; a plant with no leaves cannot collect sunlight and will not grow.
Plants need water, sunlight, and soil to live and grow; if you put a plant in a dark cupboard with water and soil, it will still not grow well because it needs sunlight.
misconceptions: A plant can grow just fine with only water; sunlight is not necessary.

core_concept: The Plant Life Cycle: From Seed to Seed
facts: To germinate, a seed needs water and warmth, but not sunlight; sunlight is needed only after the sprout appears.
After a seed germinates, a tiny root grows downward into the soil first, then a shoot pushes upward toward the light.
A flower is part of the plant that will turn into a fruit; the fruit holds the new seeds that can grow into new plants.
The plant life cycle repeats over and over: a seed grows into a plant, the plant makes flowers and then fruit with seeds, and those seeds can grow into new plants again.
misconceptions: A seed needs sunlight to begin growing into a plant.
Once a flower dies, the plant has finished its life and will never make new seeds.

core_concept: The Five Senses
facts: We use our eyes to see colors and shapes, our ears to hear sounds, our nose to smell odors, our tongue to taste flavors, and our skin to feel textures — each sense organ gives a different kind of information about an object.
An object like an apple can be identified by using multiple senses: you see its red color, feel its smooth skin, smell its fruity scent, and taste its sweetness.
What you see (sight) might not match what you feel (touch); for example, a rock that looks smooth can feel rough when you touch it.
misconceptions: A child might believe that a lemon tastes sweet because it is yellow, confusing the sense of sight (color) with the sense of taste (flavor).

core_concept: Weather and the Four Seasons
facts: Summer is the hottest season and winter is the coldest because the sun's warmth changes with the seasons, so we wear shorts in summer and coats in winter.
Spring is warmer than winter but cooler than summer; plants begin to grow as the temperature rises.
Autumn is cooler than summer but warmer than winter; leaves change color and fall from trees as the weather cools.
Weather can change from day to day; a cloudy day might bring rain, but it might also stay dry if the clouds are not rain clouds.
misconceptions: If the sky is cloudy, it always means rain will come soon.
Spring and autumn have the same weather—neither is warmer or cooler than the other.

core_concept: Empathy: Understanding and Caring About Other People's Feelings and Perspectives
facts: Empathy is understanding how someone else feels and caring about them — for example, if a classmate falls and scrapes their knee, you can imagine it hurts and want to help them feel better.

Two people in the exact same situation can have different feelings — a child who loves dogs might be happy to see a big dog, while a child who is afraid of dogs might be scared, because they have different past experiences.
Asking 'How might this feel to them?' helps you understand another person's point of view, and that understanding can lead to kind actions like giving a hug or sharing a toy.
misconceptions: A first grader might think that if they don't feel the same emotion as someone else, they don't have empathy — for example, they might believe that because they are not sad when a friend is sad, they cannot understand their friend's sadness.

core_concept: Conflict Resolution: Understanding Disagreements and Strategies for Resolving Them Fairly
facts: A conflict is a disagreement between people who want different things, and it does not mean the friendship is broken because conflicts are normal.
Listening to each person's view helps find a fair solution because you understand what each person wants and needs.
Compromise means each person gives up something they want so both can agree on a solution that is fair for everyone.
Mediation is when a neutral person helps two people in a conflict find a solution without taking sides.
misconceptions: If two people disagree, it means they are not friends anymore.
The best way to solve a conflict is to get what you want, even if the other person is unhappy.

core_concept: Community and Belonging: How Groups Build Identity, Norms, and a Sense of Membership
facts: A community's norms are the expected behaviors that help members feel safe and included; for example, taking turns talking is a norm that prevents some members from being excluded.
Belonging means feeling accepted and valued by a group; a student who raises their hand and is ignored does not feel belonging.
Exclusion, when someone is left out on purpose, harms the whole community because it breaks trust and makes members feel unsafe.
Shared identity in a community can come from a common interest, like all members loving soccer; this shared interest helps members feel like they belong together.
misconceptions: If someone is excluded, it only hurts that one person, not the group.
Norms are just rules that adults make up to control kids; they don't help anyone feel like they belong.
